problem.summary: The openssl_test_slh_dsa profile on SG2044 reports sha256_block_data_order_riscv64 as a scalar RISC-V hot path with no vector instructions.
evidence: Agent3 diagnosis diag-20260612-180518 reports duration 69.308967879 seconds, IPC 2.047, and L1 data-cache load miss rate 0.074%, suggesting a compute-bound SHA-256 path.
perf annotate for sha256_block_data_order_riscv64 shows scalar lbu, lw, sw, sllw, srlw, or, xor, and addw instruction sequences and no v* vector instructions.

The original report recommends adding -march=rv64gc_zba_zbb to linux64-riscv64, but also sets allow_auto_forward_to_agent4=false because ARM baseline, readelf Tag_RISCV_arch, and hwprobe evidence are missing.
Current OpenSSL master already includes RISC-V SHA-256 baseline, Zbb, and vector SHA-256 implementations in crypto/sha/build.info and dispatches them in crypto/sha/sha_riscv.c.
Raising the generic linux64-riscv64 -march baseline would allow compilers to emit extension instructions in ordinary C code and could break RISC-V systems without those extensions.

rootCause.summary: The observed binary selected the baseline sha256_block_data_order_riscv64 path, but current OpenSSL already has RISC-V SHA-256 Zbb and vector runtime dispatch; the safe Agent4 action is to document SHA-2 capability override diagnostics instead of changing generic RISC-V build flags.
performanceFactors: SLH-DSA SHA2 parameter sets repeatedly call SHA-256, so sha256_block_data_order_riscv64 can dominate a compute-bound profile.
crypto/sha/sha_riscv.c selects vector SHA-256 when ZVKB plus ZVKNHA or ZVKNHB and VLEN >= 128 are detected, otherwise Zbb when Zbb is detected, otherwise the baseline RV64 path.
OPENSSL_riscvcap can be used to test runtime capability selection, but it does not change compiler-selected base architecture or prove the profiled FIPS module was built with a given -march.
Documentation currently lists capability names but does not show SHA-2-specific override examples or explain the distinction between runtime dispatch diagnostics and default RISC-V target policy.

mustPreserve: Do not change default RISC-V Configure targets or force new -march extensions.
Do not change SLH-DSA, FIPS provider, digest semantics, generated assembly, or SHA-256 dispatch logic without target hardware evidence and dedicated tests.
Keep the patch behavior-neutral and limited to documentation.
Clearly prefer Linux hwprobe-based runtime detection for normal execution.
